challenges.situation: A production alert fires in a team Slack channel with 25 engineers. The alert is clearly urgent — a customer-facing service is degrading. Thirty seconds pass. No one responds. Then a minute. Then two. Finally someone asks 'Is anyone looking at this?'

options.text: The team lacks a sense of urgency — they need incident response training to build better reflexes around production issues.
options.feedback: Training won't fix a structural problem. The same engineers would respond instantly in a 3-person channel. The issue isn't that they don't care — it's that with 25 potential responders, each individual's felt obligation approaches zero. The diffusion is doing exactly what diffusion does.

options.text: The alert should auto-assign a specific named individual via on-call rotation — collapsing the diffusion by making one person unambiguously responsible.
options.feedback: Specificity defeats diffusion. When the alert says '@Maya — you own this' instead of '@channel — someone look', the cognitive escape route ('someone else will handle it') disappears. The Amish barn works because every person has a named role. On-call rotations create the same structure.

options.text: The channel is too big — splitting into smaller teams would naturally increase everyone's sense of responsibility.
options.feedback: Smaller groups help (Amazon's two-pizza principle), but the core fix is named ownership, not just reduced headcount. Even in a 5-person channel, 'Can someone look at this?' produces slower response than '@Tom — the API is returning 500s, you own payments.' Size matters, but specificity matters more.

challenges.situation: In Darley and Latané's experiments, a person having a seizure was helped 85% of the time when one person heard it, but only 31% when five people heard it. Same emergency, same ability to help.
challenges.question: What psychological mechanism explains why MORE helpers produces LESS help?

options.text: Social loafing — people put in less effort when they're part of a group because their individual contribution is less noticeable.
options.feedback: Social loafing is about effort reduction on shared tasks. This is different: people aren't doing less — they're not acting at all. The mechanism is diffusion of responsibility: each person's felt obligation shrinks in proportion to the number of other potential actors, eventually reaching zero.

options.text: Diffusion of responsibility — each person's felt obligation is inversely proportional to the number of other potential helpers, until inaction becomes rational for everyone simultaneously.
options.feedback: The cognitive chain is: 'Others know about this too → If it were serious, someone would be acting → Since no one is acting, my inaction is appropriate.' Each person's passivity validates everyone else's passivity. The result: maximum awareness, minimum action. Responsibility doesn't scale — it dissolves.

options.text: Fear of embarrassment — with more witnesses, the social cost of acting incorrectly (looking foolish) increases, so people hold back.
options.feedback: Evaluation apprehension is one factor but not the primary one. Even in anonymous experiments (where no one can see who acts), the effect persists. The dominant mechanism is diffusion: 'With five people here, my individual obligation approaches zero.' It's a math problem, not a courage problem.

challenges.front: The solution to diffused responsibility is to appeal to people's sense of teamwork and collective ownership — encourage everyone to 'own' the shared space.
challenges.back: Appeals to collective ownership actually worsen diffusion. 'We all own this' = no one owns this, because every individual can assume another member of 'we' will act. The fix is the opposite: assign INDIVIDUAL ownership of EVERY piece of the shared space. Named responsibility defeats diffusion. The Amish barn works because each person has a specific, visible role — not because they feel general 'collective ownership.'

challenges.context: Two companies both have a code quality problem — shared codebases are degrading over time.

scenarioA.label: Shared ownership
scenarioA.text: 'The whole team owns code quality.' In practice, nobody refactors because everyone assumes someone else will. Tech debt accumulates. Each quarter a VP gives a speech about 'collective responsibility.' Nothing changes because ownership of everything is ownership of nothing.

scenarioB.label: Named ownership
scenarioB.text: Every module has exactly one named owner (rotated quarterly). That person's performance review includes module health metrics. When something degrades, there's a specific person whose reputation is on the line. Code quality improves — not because these engineers care more, but because their individual contribution is visible and attributed.

challenges.explanation: Responsibility doesn't scale — it diffuses. 'The team owns quality' means no individual bears the cost of inaction. Named ownership makes contribution visible and absence noticeable — the same mechanism that makes the Amish barn-raising work. Structure beats appeals every time.

challenges.instruction: Order these from most to least vulnerable to diffusion of responsibility:
items: '@Maya is on-call; @Tom owns payments; @Sara owns auth'
correctOrder: '@Maya is on-call; @Tom owns payments; @Sara owns auth'

principle.text: When ownership belongs to everyone, it belongs to no one.
principle.subtext: Responsibility doesn't scale — it diffuses. The fix is structural: named individuals, visible contribution, and specificity that collapses the 'someone else will handle it' escape route.

reflection: Where in your life is something degrading because 'everyone' owns it? What would change if exactly one person's name was attached — and what stops you from making that person yourself?
